name: Venipuncture
description: Percutaneous puncture of a vein to obtain blood samples or establish intravenous access.
indications: Blood sampling, IV medication administration, transfusions, IV fluid therapy.
contraindications: Avoid ipsilateral arm after mastectomy, AV fistula limbs, sites with active infection, thrombosed veins.
steps: Apply tourniquet 5–10 cm proximal to intended puncture site.
Select vein — antecubital fossa preferred (median cubital, cephalic, basilic).
Clean site with 70% isopropyl alcohol; allow 30 seconds to dry.
Anchor vein with non-dominant thumb; insert needle bevel-up at 15–30° angle.
Advance until flash-back of blood appears in hub.
Collect required volume; release tourniquet before withdrawing needle.
Apply direct pressure for 2–3 minutes; apply adhesive dressing.
complications: Haematoma, haemolysis of sample, nerve injury, phlebitis, arterial puncture, vasovagal syncope.
notes: If collecting multiple tubes, use standard order of draw: blood culture, coagulation (blue), serum (red/gold), heparin (green), EDTA (purple/pink), fluoride (grey).

name: Peripheral IV Insertion
description: Placement of a short catheter into a peripheral vein to establish intravenous access for fluids or medications.
indications: IV fluid resuscitation, medication administration, blood product transfusion, contrast injection.
contraindications: Post-mastectomy ipsilateral arm, AV fistula limb, infected or damaged skin at site, thrombosed vein.
steps: Select catheter gauge appropriate to clinical need (18G general use, 14–16G for resuscitation).
Apply tourniquet; identify target vein.
Clean with chlorhexidine or alcohol swab; allow to dry.
Insert catheter-over-needle bevel-up at 15–20° with skin traction distally.
On blood flash-back, lower angle and advance 2–3 mm further.
Slide catheter off needle while stabilising; release tourniquet.
Remove needle, attach extension set or cap, flush with 5–10 mL normal saline.
Secure with transparent dressing; label with date and time.
complications: Phlebitis, infiltration, haematoma, catheter-associated bloodstream infection, air embolism.
notes: Rotate site every 72–96 hours per institutional policy. Use largest-bore catheter the vein tolerates for resuscitation.

name: Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Collection
description: Collection of arterial blood, typically from the radial artery, for blood gas and acid-base analysis.
indications: Assessment of oxygenation, ventilation, and acid-base status; monitoring ventilated patients.
contraindications: Negative modified Allen's test (radial), local infection, coagulopathy (relative), AV fistula in that limb.
steps: Perform modified Allen's test: occlude both radial and ulnar arteries, release ulnar — hand should flush within 5–7 seconds.
Position wrist in dorsiflexion (10–15°) with small roll under wrist.
Clean site with chlorhexidine; allow to dry.
Palpate radial artery and insert pre-heparinised ABG syringe at 30–45° bevel-up.
Allow blood to fill syringe passively (0.5–1 mL minimum); do not aspirate.
Withdraw needle; apply firm pressure for at least 5 minutes (longer if anticoagulated).
Expel air bubbles immediately; cap syringe and mix gently.
Transport on ice if analysis delayed > 15 minutes.
complications: Haematoma, radial artery thrombosis, nerve injury, infection, vasospasm.
notes: Femoral and brachial arteries are alternatives when radial access is not possible. Brachial lacks good collateral circulation — use with caution.

name: Urinary Catheterisation (Foley)
description: Insertion of an indwelling urethral catheter to drain the bladder continuously.
indications: Acute urinary retention, hourly urine output monitoring, perioperative management, neurogenic bladder.
contraindications: Suspected urethral injury (blood at meatus, perineal bruising, high-riding prostate on PR exam) — urological review required before catheterisation.
steps: Explain procedure; maintain patient dignity with appropriate draping.
Open catheter pack using aseptic technique.
Clean urethral meatus with antiseptic solution using circular motion (female) or proximal-to-distal (male).
Apply sterile lubricating gel into urethra (male); lubricate catheter tip (female).
Insert catheter with non-dominant hand holding penis or labia.
Advance until urine flows; in males advance fully to bifurcation before inflating balloon.
Inflate balloon with 10 mL sterile water per label; withdraw gently until resistance felt.
Connect to closed drainage bag; secure catheter to thigh.
complications: Urethral trauma, false passage, UTI, bladder spasm, paraphimosis (males), bypassing.
notes: Choose smallest catheter that meets clinical need (12–14 Fr general use). Re-assess daily and remove as soon as clinically appropriate.

name: Nasogastric Tube Insertion
description: Passage of a flexible tube through the nostril and oesophagus into the stomach for feeding, decompression, or medication administration.
indications: Enteral feeding, gastric decompression, drug administration when oral route unavailable, lavage.
contraindications: Basal skull fracture (consider orogastric route), oesophageal obstruction or recent surgery, severe coagulopathy (relative), facial trauma.
steps: Estimate insertion length: measure from nostril to ear to xiphisternum; mark with tape.
Position patient upright or with head of bed at 45°.
Lubricate tube tip; gently advance along floor of nasal passage.
Ask patient to sip water and swallow as tube passes pharynx.
Advance to pre-measured mark; secure temporarily to nose with tape.
Confirm placement: pH test of aspirate (pH ≤ 5.5 acceptable), CXR if pH inconclusive.
Secure with adhesive dressing; document insertion length and confirmation method.
complications: Pulmonary misplacement (feeding into lung — fatal if undetected), epistaxis, oesophageal perforation, sinusitis, patient discomfort.
notes: Never feed before confirming placement. CXR is gold standard. Never use auscultation ('whoosh' test) as sole confirmation method.

name: Lumbar Puncture
description: Needle insertion into the subarachnoid space at the lumbar level to collect cerebrospinal fluid or measure CSF pressure.
indications: Suspected meningitis/encephalitis, subarachnoid haemorrhage (CT-negative), CSF pressure measurement, intrathecal drug administration.
contraindications: Raised intracranial pressure with mass effect on CT, posterior fossa lesion, coagulopathy (INR > 1.5 or platelets < 50), infection at puncture site, spinal cord compression.
steps: CT head before LP if focal neurology, papilloedema, immunocompromised, or recent seizures.
Position patient in lateral decubitus with knees drawn to chest, or seated leaning forward.
Identify L3/L4 interspace (level of iliac crests).
Clean with antiseptic; apply sterile drape; infiltrate local anaesthetic.
Insert spinal needle bevel-up in midline, angling slightly cephalad.
Advance until 'give' is felt as ligamentum flavum is penetrated; remove stylet.
Attach manometer; measure opening pressure (normal 8–20 cmH₂O).
Collect 3–4 sequential tubes (1–2 mL each); send as instructed.
Re-insert stylet before withdrawing needle; apply plaster.
complications: Post-LP headache (20%), spinal haematoma, infection, herniation (if CI violated), nerve root injury.
notes: Atraumatic (pencil-point) needles significantly reduce post-LP headache. Lying flat post-procedure does not reliably prevent headache.

name: Endotracheal Intubation
description: Placement of an endotracheal tube through the larynx into the trachea to secure the airway and facilitate mechanical ventilation.
indications: Airway protection (GCS ≤ 8), respiratory failure, facilitation of mechanical ventilation, impending airway obstruction.
contraindications: There are no absolute contraindications when airway protection is needed. Relative: anticipated difficult airway (consider awake fibreoptic intubation).
steps: Pre-oxygenate with 100% O₂ for 3–5 minutes (non-rebreather mask or BVM).
Position patient: sniffing position (head extended, neck flexed).
Draw up drugs: induction agent (propofol, ketamine, or thiopentone) + suxamethonium or rocuronium (RSI).
Administer pre-oxygenation drugs; apply cricoid pressure if RSI.
Hold laryngoscope in left hand; insert blade along right side of tongue, sweeping tongue left.
Lift laryngoscope up-and-away (not back) to expose cords.
Insert ETT through cords to 21–23 cm at teeth for adults.
Inflate cuff (10 mL air); confirm placement with waveform capnography + 5-point auscultation.
Secure tube; arrange post-intubation CXR.
complications: Oesophageal intubation (unrecognised fatal), right mainstem intubation, dental damage, laryngospasm, hypotension (post-intubation), aspiration.
notes: Waveform capnography is the only reliable method to confirm tracheal placement. ETCO₂ ≥ 35 mmHg over 6 breaths confirms tracheal placement.

name: Bag-Valve-Mask Ventilation
description: Provision of positive-pressure ventilation using a self-inflating bag, one-way valve, and face mask without an advanced airway.
indications: Respiratory arrest, apnoea, inadequate ventilation prior to intubation, transport ventilation as bridge measure.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in a life-threatening situation. Relative: known oesophageal obstruction (risk of gastric inflation).
steps: Select correctly sized mask (bridges nose to chin, no eye coverage).
Tilt head back (jaw thrust if cervical spine concern); open airway.
Apply mask to face using E-C clamp technique with dominant hand.
Create airtight seal — index finger and thumb form 'C' on mask; remaining fingers lift mandible forming 'E'.
Squeeze bag with non-dominant hand to deliver tidal volume (~500 mL; visible chest rise).
Ventilate at 10–12 breaths/min in non-arrested patients; 30:2 ratio during CPR.
Watch for chest rise; avoid over-ventilation and gastric insufflation.
complications: Gastric distension and aspiration, barotrauma, inadequate seal causing hypoventilation.
notes: Two-person technique (one holding mask, one squeezing bag) significantly improves tidal volume delivery and seal. Use OPA or NPA adjuncts to maintain airway patency.

name: Needle Decompression (Tension Pneumothorax)
description: Emergency decompression of a tension pneumothorax by inserting a large-bore needle into the pleural space.
indications: Clinical diagnosis of tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, haemodynamic compromise, distended neck veins). Do NOT wait for CXR.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications given life-threatening nature. Avoid incorrect side — confirm laterality.
steps: Identify 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (MCL) on the affected side — OR 4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line (AAL) if chest wall thickness may be an issue.
Clean site rapidly with swab if time permits.
Insert 14G (or largest available) IV cannula perpendicular to skin, over superior border of rib (avoids neurovascular bundle).
Advance until resistance decreases and rush of air confirms entry.
Remove needle stylet; leave cannula in place.
Immediate clinical improvement (BP rise, SpO₂ improvement) confirms diagnosis.
Proceed to definitive chest tube insertion once stabilised.
complications: Failure to decompress (wall too thick for standard cannula length), iatrogenic pneumothorax on wrong side, laceration of lung or intercostal vessels.
notes: A 14G 8 cm cannula is required for adequate decompression in most adults. Standard 4.5 cm cannulas fail in up to 50% of obese patients. 5th ICS AAL approach is preferred in current ATLS guidelines.

name: Chest Tube Insertion
description: Placement of a drainage tube into the pleural space to drain air, blood, fluid, or pus.
indications: Pneumothorax (large or symptomatic), haemothorax, pleural effusion (significant or infected), empyema, post-thoracic surgery.
contraindications: Coagulopathy should be corrected if non-urgent. Avoid pre-existing pleural adhesions if possible.
steps: Position patient supine with arm raised behind head, rotated 30–45° to affected side.
Mark site: 4th or 5th ICS, mid-axillary line (triangle of safety: lateral edge pectoralis major, anterior edge latissimus dorsi, level of nipple).
Clean with antiseptic; apply sterile drape.
Infiltrate local anaesthetic down to and including pleura (generous volume).
Make 2–3 cm horizontal incision along superior margin of rib.
Bluntly dissect through intercostal muscles; finger-sweep pleural space.
Guide chest tube posteriorly and apically (pneumothorax) or basally (fluid/blood).
Connect to underwater seal drain or flutter valve; secure tube with sutures and dressing.
Confirm placement with CXR.
complications: Malposition, haemothorax, lung laceration, diaphragm perforation, infection, subcutaneous emphysema.
notes: Check underwater seal for swinging (confirms pleural connection) and bubbling (confirms air leak). Drain output > 200 mL/hr blood is a surgical emergency.

name: Thoracentesis
description: Needle aspiration of pleural fluid for diagnostic analysis or therapeutic relief of symptomatic effusion.
indications: Undiagnosed pleural effusion (diagnostic), large symptomatic effusion causing dyspnoea (therapeutic).
contraindications: Uncorrected coagulopathy, very small effusion (< 1 cm on ultrasound), patient unable to cooperate, overlying skin infection.
steps: Confirm effusion with bedside ultrasound; mark optimal puncture point.
Position patient seated leaning forward over a pillow-supporting table, or lateral decubitus.
Clean with antiseptic; infiltrate local anaesthetic down to pleura over superior rib margin.
Insert thoracentesis needle/catheter at 90° to skin, aspirating continuously.
On entering pleural fluid, advance catheter; remove needle.
Aspirate fluid — remove up to 1,500 mL at one session (more risks re-expansion pulmonary oedema).
Send samples: LDH, protein, glucose, cytology, pH, Gram stain and culture as indicated.
Withdraw needle; apply dressing; CXR to check for pneumothorax.
complications: Pneumothorax (5–10%), haemothorax, re-expansion pulmonary oedema (if > 1.5 L removed), pain, empyema.
notes: Ultrasound guidance reduces complication rates. Apply Light's criteria to distinguish exudate from transudate.

name: Paracentesis
description: Needle insertion into the peritoneal cavity to withdraw ascitic fluid for diagnostic analysis or therapeutic drainage.
indications: New-onset ascites (diagnostic), tense symptomatic ascites (large-volume therapeutic), suspected spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
contraindications: Disseminated intravascular coagulation, surgical abdomen, bowel obstruction with visible loops. Coagulopathy/thrombocytopaenia are relative — do not routinely correct unless severe.
steps: Confirm ascites with percussion or bedside ultrasound.
Position patient supine with slight lateral tilt toward puncture side.
Site: left or right iliac fossa, lateral third of line from umbilicus to ASIS (avoids inferior epigastric vessels and caecum).
Clean with antiseptic; infiltrate local anaesthetic into skin and peritoneum.
Insert needle/cannula with 'Z-track' technique; aspirate on entry.
On entering peritoneal fluid, secure catheter; connect to drainage bag.
For large-volume paracentesis: infuse 6–8 g albumin IV per litre drained beyond 5 L to prevent circulatory dysfunction.
Remove catheter; apply pressure dressing.
complications: Persistent ascitic leak, haemorrhage, infection, bowel perforation, hypotension (if albumin not replaced).
notes: Send samples for cell count (SBP if PMN > 250 cells/mm³), protein, albumin (to calculate SAAG), culture, cytology as clinically indicated.

name: Joint Aspiration (Arthrocentesis)
description: Needle aspiration of synovial fluid from a joint space for diagnostic analysis or therapeutic decompression.
indications: Suspected septic arthritis, crystal arthropathy (gout/pseudogout), haemarthrosis, large symptomatic effusion.
contraindications: Overlying skin infection, bacteraemia (relative — septic joint may still need aspiration), prosthetic joint (refer to orthopaedics), coagulopathy.
steps: Confirm effusion clinically; ultrasound guidance improves accuracy.
Clean skin with antiseptic; use sterile technique throughout.
Infiltrate local anaesthetic into skin and subcutaneous tissue (not into joint to avoid cell lysis).
Insert needle at standard landmark for target joint (e.g., superolateral approach for knee).
Aspirate fluid; if resistance, reposition needle slightly.
Withdraw maximum fluid (therapeutic); apply pressure dressing.
Send samples: Gram stain and culture, cell count, crystals under polarised light, glucose, protein.
complications: Iatrogenic infection, haemarthrosis, cartilage damage, nerve/vessel injury.
notes: Synovial fluid WBC > 50,000 cells/mm³ is highly suggestive of septic arthritis (treat empirically and arrange urgent surgical washout). Crystals: MSU (gout) = negatively birefringent; CPPD = weakly positively birefringent.

name: Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy
description: Sampling of bone marrow from the posterior iliac crest for haematological diagnosis.
indications: Unexplained cytopenias, haematological malignancy staging/diagnosis, fever of unknown origin with suspicion of marrow pathology, monitoring treatment response.
contraindications: Severe thrombocytopaenia (relative — proceed with caution), active skin infection overlying site, patient refusal.
steps: Position patient prone or lateral decubitus; identify posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS).
Clean broadly with antiseptic; apply sterile drape.
Infiltrate generous local anaesthetic including periosteum (this is the most painful step).
Insert aspiration needle with trocar through skin and subcutaneous tissue; advance using rotational pressure into cortex.
Remove trocar; attach 10 mL syringe; aspirate 0.5–1 mL with firm pull (patient will feel sharp pain).
Remove aspiration needle; spread marrow on slides immediately; send remainder in EDTA.
Re-insert trephine biopsy needle; advance 2–3 cm into marrow; rotate and withdraw with core intact.
Place core in formalin; apply dressing with pressure.
complications: Pain, haematoma, infection, nerve injury, rarely haemorrhage.
notes: Trephine biopsy is essential for assessing marrow architecture (e.g., myelofibrosis). PSIS preferred over sternum — provides better access and avoids cardiac injury risk.

name: Intraosseous (IO) Access
description: Placement of a needle or drill-driven device into the intramedullary cavity of a bone to provide rapid vascular access when IV access is unavailable.
indications: Life-threatening emergency requiring immediate vascular access when peripheral IV cannot be established within 2 attempts or 90 seconds.
contraindications: Fracture in target bone, infection overlying site, previous IO in same bone within 24–48 hours, osteopetrosis.
steps: Select site: proximal tibia (2 cm below tibial tuberosity, medial surface) preferred in adults and children. Humeral head or distal tibia are alternatives.
Clean site; apply local anaesthetic if patient conscious.
Insert IO needle/EZ-IO device perpendicular to bone with firm pressure + rotational motion until 'give' felt as cortex is penetrated.
Remove stylet; confirm position by aspiration of marrow (dark blood) or resistance to flush.
Flush with 10 mL normal saline (may be painful in conscious patients).
Attach IV line; secure device with dressing and arm board.
Remove within 24 hours; establish conventional IV access as soon as possible.
complications: Extravasation/compartment syndrome (most serious), infection, fat embolism, tibial fracture, growth plate damage (paediatrics).
notes: IO route provides equivalent drug delivery and onset compared to IV. All resuscitation drugs, fluids, and blood products can be given via IO.

name: Central Venous Catheter Insertion
description: Placement of a multi-lumen catheter into a central vein (internal jugular, subclavian, or femoral) under ultrasound guidance.
indications: Administration of vasopressors or concentrated medications, central venous pressure monitoring, long-term IV access, haemodialysis, poor peripheral access.
contraindications: Uncorrected coagulopathy (relative), SVC obstruction, anatomical anomaly at intended site. Subclavian avoided with severe coagulopathy (non-compressible).
steps: Obtain informed consent; position patient appropriately (Trendelenburg for IJ/subclavian).
Real-time ultrasound to identify vein and confirm compressibility.
Apply full sterile barrier precautions (cap, mask, gown, gloves, large drape).
Anaesthetise skin and tissue tract with lignocaine.
Under ultrasound guidance, insert introducer needle, confirm venous return (non-pulsatile, dark blood).
Insert guidewire through needle; confirm position under ultrasound; remove needle.
Nick skin; advance dilator over wire; remove dilator.
Advance catheter over wire to appropriate depth; remove wire.
Confirm all lumens aspirate and flush; suture catheter; apply occlusive dressing.
CXR to confirm tip position (SVC/RA junction) and exclude pneumothorax.
complications: Arterial puncture, pneumothorax, haemothorax, air embolism, catheter-related bloodstream infection, arrhythmia, thrombosis.
notes: Ultrasound guidance is standard of care for IJ insertion (reduces arterial puncture by 70%). Maximal sterile barrier technique reduces CRBSI. Daily review and prompt removal when no longer clinically required.

name: Pericardiocentesis
description: Needle aspiration of fluid from the pericardial sac, typically under echocardiographic or fluoroscopic guidance, to relieve cardiac tamponade.
indications: Cardiac tamponade with haemodynamic compromise, large symptomatic pericardial effusion, diagnostic sampling.
contraindications: Aortic dissection (may extend), haemorrhagic effusion requiring surgical drainage, small or loculated effusions (relative — guided approach preferred).
steps: Continuous ECG and haemodynamic monitoring; IV access established.
Echocardiography to confirm effusion location and size.
Position patient 30–45° head-up.
Site: subxiphoid approach — 1–2 cm below xiphoid, aiming toward left shoulder at 45°.
Clean with antiseptic; anaesthetise skin and subcutaneous tissue.
Insert needle while aspirating; confirm pericardial entry by return of fluid.
ECG monitoring throughout — ST changes or widening QRS indicates myocardial contact; withdraw slightly.
Thread guidewire if indwelling drain planned; aspirate to dryness.
Send fluid for cytology, culture, protein, glucose, LDH.
complications: Cardiac laceration, ventricular puncture, pneumothorax, haemopericardium, arrhythmia, coronary artery injury.
notes: Beck's triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins) classically describes tamponade. Echocardiographic guidance reduces complication rate significantly.

name: Synchronised DC Cardioversion
description: Delivery of a timed electrical shock synchronised to the R wave to terminate haemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias.
indications: Haemodynamically unstable atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, SVT, or VT with a pulse. Elective cardioversion for AF/flutter.
contraindications: VF or pulseless VT (use unsynchronised defibrillation), digoxin toxicity (relative), known sinus node dysfunction.
steps: Confirm rhythm and haemodynamic status; obtain IV access.
Elective cases: ensure anticoagulation for ≥ 3 weeks if AF > 48 hours, or TEE to exclude thrombus.
Sedate/anaesthetise patient (propofol, midazolam, or ketamine with anaesthetic support).
Attach pads in anterior-posterior or anterior-lateral position.
Engage SYNC mode — confirm R-wave markers are visible on monitor.
Select energy: AF 100–200 J biphasic; flutter 50–100 J; SVT 50–100 J; VT 100–200 J.
Ensure all clear; deliver shock — may require second shock at higher energy.
Confirm rhythm restoration; monitor for minimum 30 minutes post-cardioversion.
complications: Skin burns, transient ST changes, bradycardia/sinus arrest, systemic embolism (AF), deterioration to VF (desynchronisation).
notes: Always use SYNC mode for tachyarrhythmias with a pulse. Failing to sync in AF can precipitate VF. Unsynchronised defibrillation is used only for VF and pulseless VT.

name: Cricothyrotomy
description: Emergency incision through the cricothyroid membrane to establish a surgical airway when conventional intubation fails.
indications: Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate (CICO) situation. Last resort when BVM, supraglottic airway, and intubation have all failed.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in a CICO emergency. Age < 8–10 years: needle cricothyrotomy preferred as first-line surgical airway.
steps: Identify cricothyroid membrane by palpating between thyroid and cricoid cartilages.
Stabilise larynx with left hand; make vertical skin incision 3–4 cm over CTM.
Make horizontal stab incision through CTM (inferior third to avoid cricothyroid arteries).
Hook index finger into incision; dilate with curved haemostat or tracheal hook.
Insert cuffed ETT (6.0) or dedicated cricothyrotomy device.
Inflate cuff; ventilate; confirm with waveform capnography.
Secure tube; arrange definitive tracheostomy within 72 hours.
complications: Oesophageal placement, haemorrhage, subglottic stenosis, tube displacement, posterior tracheal wall injury.
notes: Scalpel-finger-bougie technique is now standard (ATLS, DAS guidelines). Practise on mannequins regularly — this is a high-stakes, low-frequency procedure.

name: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)
description: Structured sequence of chest compressions and rescue breaths to maintain perfusion in cardiac arrest until ROSC or cessation of efforts.
indications: Cardiac arrest (absence of breathing and pulse). Begin immediately; do not delay for monitoring.
contraindications: DNACPR order in place, obviously non-survivable injury, signs of irreversible death (rigor mortis, dependent lividity, decapitation).
steps: Check safety; approach and confirm unresponsive; call for help and AED.
Open airway (head tilt/chin lift or jaw thrust); check for breathing for no more than 10 seconds.
If no breathing: begin 30 chest compressions (100–120/min, 5–6 cm depth, full recoil, minimal interruptions).
Give 2 rescue breaths (1 second each, visible chest rise) via BVM or mouth-to-mouth.
Continue 30:2 ratio until AED/defibrillator available.
Analyse rhythm: if shockable (VF/pulseless VT), deliver shock and immediately resume CPR.
Continue 2-minute cycles; give adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO every 3–5 minutes.
Amiodarone 300 mg IV after 3rd shock for refractory VF/pVT.
Search for and treat reversible causes (4Hs and 4Ts).
complications: Rib/sternal fractures, haemopneumothorax, liver laceration (rarely), aspiration.
notes: 4Hs: Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypo/Hyperkalaemia, Hypothermia. 4Ts: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thromboembolism. High-quality compressions and early defibrillation are the interventions with strongest evidence for improving survival.

name: Arterial Line Insertion
description: Placement of an indwelling arterial cannula, typically in the radial artery, for continuous blood pressure monitoring and arterial blood sampling.
indications: Haemodynamically unstable patients requiring continuous BP monitoring, frequent ABG sampling, vasopressor titration, major surgery.
contraindications: Failed Allen's test (radial), infection at site, Raynaud's disease (relative), severe coagulopathy (relative).
steps: Perform modified Allen's test; position wrist in dorsiflexion.
Palpate radial artery; clean with antiseptic.
Use real-time ultrasound or Seldinger technique depending on approach.
Infiltrate local anaesthetic into skin if patient awake.
Insert 20G cannula bevel-up at 30–45°; on pulsatile blood return, lower angle and advance.
Thread guidewire through cannula; remove cannula, nick skin, advance arterial catheter over wire.
Remove wire; attach to pressurised flush system and transducer.
Secure and label cannula; zero transducer at mid-axillary line.
complications: Thrombosis and distal ischaemia, haematoma, infection, pseudoaneurysm, vasospasm.
notes: Site ≤ 5 days to reduce infection risk. Femoral and brachial arteries are alternatives but carry higher complication rates. Brachial lacks collateral circulation — use with caution.

name: Wound Closure (Suturing)
description: Approximation of wound edges with sutures to promote primary intention healing.
indications: Lacerations amenable to primary closure: clean wounds, < 6–8 hours old (face up to 12–24 hours), adequate tissue available.
contraindications: Heavily contaminated wounds, bite wounds (relative — face can be sutured), wounds > 8–12 hours old in high-risk areas, signs of infection.
steps: Irrigate wound copiously with normal saline (minimum 250 mL per cm of wound).
Debride devitalised tissue and foreign material.
Infiltrate local anaesthetic (lignocaine ± adrenaline — avoid adrenaline in digits, penis, nose, ear).
Explore wound for structure involvement (tendon, nerve, vessel).
Close deep layers with absorbable suture (Vicryl) to eliminate dead space.
Approximate skin edges with non-absorbable suture (Prolene, Ethilon) or skin staples.
Apply non-adherent dressing; advise wound care instructions and review date.
Prescribe antibiotics only if high-risk wound (bite, contaminated, immunocompromised).
complications: Infection, wound dehiscence, haematoma, hypertrophic/keloid scar, stitch abscess.
notes: Suture removal times: face 5 days, scalp 7–10 days, trunk 10–14 days, extremities 10–14 days, joints 14 days. Verify tetanus immunisation status.

name: Incision and Drainage (I&D)
description: Surgical incision of a fluctuant abscess to drain purulent material and promote healing.
indications: Fluctuant cutaneous abscess. Empirical antibiotics alone are insufficient — drainage is required.
contraindications: Non-fluctuant cellulitis (no pus to drain), pilonidal sinus (needs formal excision), Bartholin's cyst/abscess (refer to gynaecology), infections overlying prostheses.
steps: Confirm fluctuance clinically; ultrasound helps if uncertain.
Clean skin with antiseptic; infiltrate local anaesthetic around (not into) abscess.
Make incision along Langer's lines or natural skin creases, over point of maximum fluctuance.
Break up loculations with haemostat.
Irrigate cavity with normal saline.
Pack loosely with ribbon gauze if large cavity (to prevent premature closure); review at 48 hours.
Send pus for Gram stain, culture and sensitivity.
Antibiotics only if: cellulitis > 2 cm around abscess, systemic features, immunocompromised, or MRSA risk.
complications: Recurrence, wound healing by secondary intention (acceptable), fistula formation, inadequate drainage.
notes: MRSA is a common causative organism for community-acquired skin abscesses. Consider co-trimoxazole or doxycycline if MRSA risk is high and antibiotics are indicated.

name: Skin Punch Biopsy
description: Cylindrical full-thickness skin biopsy using a circular cutting instrument for histological diagnosis of skin lesions.
indications: Undiagnosed skin lesions, suspected malignancy, chronic or inflammatory dermatoses requiring histological diagnosis.
contraindications: Bleeding diathesis (relative), hypersensitivity to local anaesthetic, lesions in high-risk anatomical locations (eyelid — refer to specialist).
steps: Select appropriate punch size (3–4 mm standard; 6 mm for larger lesion or inflammatory disease).
Select biopsy site: representative area of lesion (centre for tumours, edge for inflammatory conditions).
Clean skin with alcohol; infiltrate lignocaine ± adrenaline into subcutaneous fat.
Stretch skin perpendicular to relaxed skin tension lines (RSTL).
Press punch firmly and rotate in one direction until hub contacts skin.
Lift core with needle or forceps (avoid crushing); cut base with scissors.
Place specimen in formalin; if fresh tissue required, place in saline.
Achieve haemostasis: pressure, single interrupted suture, or silver nitrate.
Apply non-adherent dressing; remove sutures at 7–10 days.
complications: Bleeding, infection, scarring, inadequate sample (insufficient depth), nerve damage.
notes: Stretching skin perpendicular to RSTL produces an elliptical defect aligned with skin tension lines when tension is released, resulting in a neater scar. Send clinical details including suspected diagnosis on request form.

name: Digital Nerve Block
description: Regional anaesthesia of a finger or toe by injecting local anaesthetic around the digital nerves at the base of the digit.
indications: Laceration repair, nail avulsion, fracture manipulation, abscess drainage, or foreign body removal involving a finger or toe.
contraindications: Known allergy to local anaesthetic. NEVER use adrenaline-containing solutions in digital blocks — risk of ischaemia.
steps: Draw up plain lignocaine (1–2%) WITHOUT adrenaline.
Insert needle at dorsal aspect of the MCP/MTP joint on one side of the digit.
Advance needle to palmar/plantar surface while injecting 1–2 mL as needle traverses.
Withdraw slightly; inject another 0.5 mL at dorsal surface to block dorsal branch.
Repeat on opposite side of digit for complete block.
Wait 5–10 minutes for block to take effect before starting procedure.
Test with blunt probe prior to commencing.
complications: Failed block, haematoma, infection, intravascular injection.
notes: Web space block (injecting at the web space between two digits) achieves the same effect with fewer needle passes for some practitioners. Maximum dose: plain lignocaine 3 mg/kg.

name: Nasopharyngeal Airway (NPA) Insertion
description: Insertion of a soft flexible tube through the nostril into the pharynx to maintain airway patency in a semi-conscious patient.
indications: Airway obstruction in obtunded patient who has intact gag reflex (where OPA is not tolerated), as adjunct during BVM ventilation.
contraindications: Suspected basal skull fracture, severe coagulopathy (relative), nasal obstruction.
steps: Select size: approximate diameter equals patient's little finger; length from nostril to tragus of ear.
Apply safety pin through flange (prevents migration distally).
Lubricate tube generously with water-soluble gel.
Insert bevel toward nasal septum; advance gently along floor of nasal passage.
If resistance felt, try other nostril — do not force.
Confirm patency by listening/feeling for airflow through tube.
Secure with tape to cheek if prolonged use anticipated.
complications: Epistaxis (most common), submucosal passage, laryngospasm (if inserted too far), aspiration.
notes: Better tolerated than OPA in semi-conscious patients. Useful as airway adjunct during BVM ventilation where mask seal is poor. Lubricate generously to minimise epistaxis.

name: Oropharyngeal Airway (OPA) Insertion
description: Insertion of a rigid curved plastic airway to maintain oro-pharyngeal patency by displacing the tongue from the posterior pharynx.
indications: Unconscious patient without gag reflex (GCS ≤ 8) with airway obstruction due to tongue falling back; adjunct during BVM ventilation.
contraindications: Intact gag reflex (will cause vomiting and laryngospasm), oral trauma or foreign body, trismus.
steps: Select size: measure from centre of mouth to angle of jaw (approximately).
In adults: insert upside down (concave up), rotate 180° once past teeth, or use tongue depressor method.
In children: use tongue depressor to press tongue down and insert OPA in correct orientation (do not rotate — risk of palate injury).
Confirm correct position: improved airflow, jaw lift may no longer be needed, tongue not pushed back.
Reassess airway patency after insertion.
complications: Vomiting and aspiration (if gag reflex present), dental damage, laryngospasm, trauma to soft palate.
notes: OPA is a temporising measure — does not protect against aspiration. Airway should be definitively secured with ETT if prolonged ventilation or airway protection is needed.
